title: Humanist minuscule, the Renaissance bookhand
subtitle: The fifteenth-century Italian hand that gave us modern lowercase type
excerpt: Humanist minuscule was developed in Florence in the early fifteenth century by scribes influenced by Carolingian manuscripts. It is an upright, rounded hand with serifs and moderate pen angle. Most modern roman typefaces: Times New Roman, Garamond, Palatino: derive their lowercase forms from humanist minuscule.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), public domain, Internet Archive. Johnston traces the Foundational hand through Carolingian to the humanist scribes; his pen-hold and letter-proportion analysis applies to humanist minuscule directly.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fifteenth-century Italian bookhand based on a careful study of Carolingian manuscripts by Renaissance humanist scholars. Upright, rounded, and serifed; the direct predecessor of roman typeface lowercase forms.

content.text: Florentine humanists of the 1420s were dissatisfied with the gothic hands of their time. They studied Carolingian manuscripts and produced a script called "littera antiqua": the old letter. This
is upright and round, with a 30° pen angle. It looks familiar: roman typeface designers used humanist manuscripts as their models. Aldus Manutius was among them.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 3 (slug: nib-mitchell-roundhand-3)
items.description: At 4.5-5 nib-widths for the x-height, the humanist minuscule body is approximately 6-7 mm at nib size 3.

items.name: Straight pen holder (slug: straight-pen-holder)
items.description: Humanist minuscule uses a straight holder at a 30° pen angle.

content.text: The pen angle is 30°, the same as Foundational. The x-height is slightly shorter: 4.5-5 nib-widths rather than 4. Ascenders and descenders are longer: about 6-7 nib-widths for ascenders. The letterforms are slightly narrower than Foundational. The serifs are more horizontal: thin entry and exit strokes rather than the rounded Foundational foot.

a: the two-story a (not the single-story a of Foundational). Stroke 1: the bowl, a compressed oval on the right side. Stroke 2: the vertical stem descending from the top of the bowl, with a serif at the head. Stroke 3: the foot, a curved entry into the bowl from the stem base. This is the feature most readers associate with printed type; practise it first.

b: ascender stroke (stroke 1) with a bowl attached at the base right (stroke 2). The bowl in humanist minuscule is slightly smaller than the Foundational b: closer to the proportions of a modern roman b. d: bowl first (stroke 1), then ascender (stroke 2) rising from the top right of the bowl. The ascender is straight and tall; the serif at the head is a fine horizontal stroke. e: a curved stroke that does not close at the tail; the crossbar is a short horizontal cut. f: a descending stroke with a crossbar; the foot curves left below the baseline. g: two-story: bowl (stroke 1), link stroke (stroke 2), lower bowl descender (stroke 3). The link is a subtle feature; in humanist manuscripts it is sometimes missing or minimal.

h: an ascender with an arched right branch joining at x-height. The foot of the right branch curves right. i: a vertical with a horizontal serif at both head and foot; the dot is a single nib-stroke square. n: two strokes: left vertical and right branch. The arch is the same as h. p: a stem descending below the baseline (stroke 1) with a right-side bowl (stroke 2) whose top is at the cap line. q: left-side bowl (stroke 1) with a descending stem (stroke 2).

The best drill is writing words that contain a, e, n, and o at the same scale. Check that the counter shapes: the interior white space: are consistent across all four letters. Variation in counters is the clearest sign of uneven pen angle or letter width.
